humanities_role_explanation: Introduces an evaluation framework grounded in art-theoretical constructs to benchmark VLM capabilities in generating expert-level art critiques and cultural interpretations across six traditions.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Combines artwork images with bilingual (Chinese-English) natural language prompts and model-generated critiques to evaluate cultural art interpretation.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates models across a five-level hierarchy: visual perception (L1), technical analysis (L2), cultural symbolism (L3), historical context (L4), and philosophical aesthetics (L5), moving from basic pre-iconographic description to deep iconological interpretation.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Specifically designed for evaluating domain-specific cultural competence and art-historical interpretation rather than general-purpose abilities.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates 15 vision-language models including GPT-5, GPT-5.2, GPT-4o, Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Claude-Opus-4.5, Claude-Haiku-3.5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Gemini-3-Pro, Qwen3-VL-235B, Llama4-Scout, and DeepSeek-VL2.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary systems being benchmarked are generative Vision-Language Models (VLMs) acting as critique generators.

arts_humanities_data.note: Covers Visual Arts and Performing Arts (ASJC 1213), Art History, Philosophy (Aesthetics), and Literature across Chinese, Western, Japanese, Korean, Islamic, and Indian traditions.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses multi-dimensional rubric-based scores (Coverage, Alignment, Depth, Accuracy, Quality), automated metrics (DCR, CSA, CDS, LQS), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and ICC(2,1).

english_language.note: The paper and evaluation framework are written in English, using bilingual (English and Chinese) critiques.

summary: The paper presents VULCA-BENCH, a tri-tier evaluation framework for cross-cultural, expert-level art critique generation in Vision-Language Models. Grounded in Panofsky's and Goodman's theories of art interpretation, it maps critiques to a 5-level analytical depth hierarchy and leverages a single-judge calibration strategy to align model-based evaluation with human expert ratings. The framework is applied to 15 major VLMs across six distinct cultural traditions to diagnose systematic degradation in interpretive depth and cultural biases.
key_findings: VLM cultural competence degrades significantly from surface-level visual description (L1-L2) to deep cultural interpretation (L3-L5), with Depth and Alignment serving as the most discriminative dimensions. In addition, 13 of 15 evaluated VLMs display systematic Western bias, scoring Western art significantly higher than non-Western traditions (Cohen's d = -0.74, p < 0.001). Automated metrics and dual-judge averaging are highly unreliable (ICC < 0.2), validating the paper's single-judge sigmoid calibration which successfully aligns automated outputs with human experts (reducing held-out MAE to 0.446).
